channel.title: Posts on ChrisDewa's blog
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on ChrisDewa's blog

item.title: Oop isn't weird Grupert
item.description: Yeah, it isn’t THAT weird OOP stands for “Object Oriented Programming” and it is a programming paradigm in which you write your code into encapsulated blocks of code called classes which are basically blueprints that determine how objects behave. Objects are then bits of code that have a clear purpose and structure and they can access specific data and methods. Let’s think of an everyday object, a door. A door has many attributes, for example it’s color, it’s material, maybe it’s weight, it also has a property, is it open or closed, and it has a method, open or close.
item.content:
Yeah, it isn’t THAT weird

OOP stands for “Object Oriented Programming” and it is a programming paradigm in which you write your code into encapsulated blocks of code called classes which are basically blueprints that determine how objects behave.

Objects are then bits of code that have a clear purpose and structure and they can access specific data and methods. Let’s think of an everyday object, a door. A door has many attributes, for example it’s color, it’s material, maybe it’s weight, it also has a property, is it open or closed, and it has a method, open or close. Let’s compare two paradigms, procedural vs OOP with this example:

procedural

def make_door(name, color, material):
    door = {
        'name':name, 'color': color, 'material': material, 'status': 'closed'
    }

def open_door(door):
    door['status'] = 'opened'

def close_door(door):
    door['status'] = 'closed'

def is_door_open(door):
    return door['status'] = 'opened'

my_door = make_door('front door', 'red', 'wood')

OOP

class Door:
    def __init__(name, color, material):
        self.name = name
        self.color = color
        self.material = material
        self.status = 'closed'
    
    @property
    def is_opened(self):
        return self.status == 'opened'
    
    def open(self):
        self.status = 'opened'
    
    def close(self):
        self.status = 'closed'

my_door = Door('front door', 'red', 'wood')

As you can see both codes are easily legible and maintainable. OOP encapsulates all the logic pertaining to an object within it’s class. Procedural defines blocks of selfcontained logic that are reusable and minimalistic. And both are adecuate when programming. But then, what’s the point of this overly complicated OOP stuff? Well, let’s say you want to expand the code, so that this is not just some abstract weird door, but a part of a car. In OOP it might be as simple as extending the class thorough inheritance. Inheritance means that a class gets some base parameters from another class, and then you can expand on it, say we just want to add an electrical window to our door.

class CarWindow:
    ... # we define the logic for the window here


class CarDoor(Door):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kws):
        # this just means "do whatever you were doing within the Door class"
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        # and then add the new property
        self.drivers_window = CarWindow(...)

Above we see something new, super what it does in simple terms is executing the method of the base class, in this case Door. So what we’re doing is simply doing the same thing Door is doing but also adding a driver’s window, which is a class istance itself. A subclass can override methods or attributes of the base class if needed to change how they work or extend their functionality.

Let’s see another example of this simple inheritance.

class Animal:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
    
    def talk(self):
        raise Exception('talk methos has not been implemented')

class Dog(Animal):
    def talk(self):
        print('woof')

class Cat(Animal):
    def talk(self):
        print('miau')

In here we’re not doing any weird things, we define a base class, with a method talk which raises an exception because it’s not implemented yet. This kinds of classes are called “base abstract classes” or “ABC” for short, and they define how a class should look like, but are not intented to make objects by themselves but instead they’re meant to be inherited from. So now, we have a Dog and a Cat that are both animals and both can “talk”, this is called polymorphism and is a key concept in OOP, it simply means that we can use the same method on multiple classes when they inherit from a base class, so we can have any animal “talk” it doesn’t matter if its a dog, a cat or mouse, as long as they’re animals, they’ll have a talk method.

And now it’s a very good time to review one of the best advantages of OOP, which is types. Imagine you’re an inventor (which if you code you kind of already are) and you invented a washing machine which can wash cats, dogs, and yes cars. Since you don’t want to kill your pets, you have to identify the method to use for washing them vs washing the cars. How you’d do it with OOP?

if isinstance(item, Animal):
    machine.wash_animal(item)
elif isinstance(item, Car):
    machine.wash_car(item)

Now think about how you’d implement this in another programming paradimg. You would first need a way to determine if the item is an animal or a car and this will implicitly add more complexity to your code, becase for every new item your machine can process, you’ll need a function to check if it’s the correct kind. This doesn’t mean it can’t be done or that it’s overly complex, but OOP excels at this kind of situations.

Any programming project must be well organized, easy to read and maintain. This is true for any paradigm and it will be different for every one, but OOP is awesome to simplify complex checks, encapsulate and manipulating data and modeling data structures.

So don’t be afraid of OOP, it’s awesome!

item.title: Fun with metaclasses
item.description: Everything in Python is an object. An object is an instance of a class, but even classes are objects themselves. It can be a bit confusing when trying to learn Object Oriented Programing (OOP) with python and one of the trickiest of subjects are metaclasses, let’s talk about them! To define a class in Python, the idiomatic way to do it is simple and I’m sure you’ve seen it before:
item.content:
Everything in Python is an object. An object is an instance of a class, but even classes are objects themselves. It can be a bit confusing when trying to learn Object Oriented Programing (OOP) with python and one of the trickiest of subjects are metaclasses, let’s talk about them!

To define a class in Python, the idiomatic way to do it is simple and I’m sure you’ve seen it before:

class Person:
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name
        self.age = age

But do you know that this is actually syntactic sugar? yeah, let me show you what’s going on behind the curtains.

Do you remember type, that “function” thing you use to check the “type” of an object? You might have used it like:

number = 42
print(type(number))
# <class 'int'>

Well type is not a function at all!! It’s real job is creating all the objects and classes you use in python. And you can actually use it for this as well. This example will be equivalent to the one above.

def __init__(self, name, age):
    self.name = name
    self.age = age
Person = type('Person', (), {'__init__': __init__})

The first argument of type used in this way (to create a class) is the name, the second is “bases”, meaning the classes it inherits from and finally the “attributes”, a dictionary with all the methods and attributes of the class instances.

What I’m trying to show here is that type (a class itself) is the maker of any other classes you might use. But… you can make class makers of your own. When you define classes, they usually inherit from object, but to make a class maker, you inherit directly from type. These “class makers” as I’ve been calling them are actually called “metaclasses”.

If a class is the blueprint of an object, a metaclass is the blueprint of classes. Metaclasses, then, control how a class is created. This is a simple example of a metaclass:

class MyMeta(type): # inherit from type
    # call method is used when calling() a class to make an object
    # here it'd be Person()
    def __call__(cls, *args, **kws): 
        print(args, kws)
        return super().__call__(*args, **kws)


class Person(metaclass=MyMeta):
    def __init__(self, name, age): 
        self.name = name
        self.age = age


chris = Person('chris', age=35)
# ('chris',) {'age': 35}

As you see inheriting from MyMeta with the keyword “metaclass” adds functionality to our base class, here it’s just some bad loggin. But the main thing is the metaclass is not altering the __call__ method of the class, it’s actually altering what happens when you use the class to create an object.

Metaclasses are rarely ever needed, but are really fun to use. One of the most asked patterns regarding metaclasses is the “singleton”. A singleton is a class that can only have one, and only one, object. The actual use is very controversial as you can read in this question in StackOverflow.

Let’s see how you’d use the pattern above to create a singleton:

class SingletonMeta(type):
    __instances = {}
    
    def __call__(cls, *args, **kws):
        return cls.__instances.setdefault(
            cls, super().__call__(*args, **kws)
        )


class Person(metaclass=SingletonMeta):
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name
        self.age = age
        
chris = Person('chris', 35)
moni = Person('moni', 36)

print(chris.name, moni.name)
print(f'{chris is moni =}')
# chris chris
# chris is moni = True

What’s happening here is that classes are hashable, they return their ID, even if their objects aren’t hashable. This lets us use the class as a key in an instances dictionary, and setdefault the first instances it creates. Any other instances of that class will actually be the same object. This class can no longer create other objects, all thanks to the metaclass.

So that’s it for this post. Metaclasses then are the blueprints of classes and they’re creating by inheriting from type instead of object. They can add some amazing functionality but for a regular use case, they’re almost never needed as there is almost always a simpler way and often times using them only adds complexity to the code, making it harder to understand and maintain. They, however, remain a very powerful tool you should master in your way to be a python master.

item.title: The zen of python
item.content:
If you want to entune yourself to python’s zen open up an interactive session and enter:

import this

This text will appear:

The Zen of Python, by Tim Peters

Beautiful is better than ugly.
Explicit is better than implicit.
Simple is better than complex.
Complex is better than complicated.
Flat is better than nested.
Sparse is better than dense.
Readability counts.
Special cases aren't special enough to break the rules.
Although practicality beats purity.
Errors should never pass silently.
Unless explicitly silenced.
In the face of ambiguity, refuse the temptation to guess.
There should be one-- and preferably only one --obvious way to do it.
Although that way may not be obvious at first unless you're Dutch.
Now is better than never.
Although never is often better than *right* now.
If the implementation is hard to explain, it's a bad idea.
If the implementation is easy to explain, it may be a good idea.
Namespaces are one honking great idea -- let's do more of those!

Read it. Learn it. Apply it!

And that’s it!
